Title: Chloroplastic ascorbate modifies plant metabolism and may act as a metabolite signal regardless of oxidative stress

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: PHT4 family transporter; chloroplast ascorbate transporter
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of PHT4;4 decreases chloroplastic ascorbate without reducing total leaf ascorbate, and causes broad metabolome changes consistent with altered chloroplastic ascorbate signaling.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transcript detected in leaves; absent in pht4;4-1 and pht4;4-3 mutants; partially retained in pht4;4-4; PHT4;2 induced at high light in pht4;4 knockouts and vtc2-4.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA mutant analysis (pht4;4-1, pht4;4-3, pht4;4-4); RT-PCR/RT-qPCR; chloroplast ascorbate quantification by HPLC; nonaqueous fractionation; PSII ascorbate electron-donation kinetics; metabolomics profiling.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Normal light (~80 µmol photons m^-2 s^-1); high light (~800 µmol photons m^-2 s^-1 for 2 days).
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PHT4;4 is required for normal chloroplastic ascorbate accumulation. 2. pht4;4-3 chloroplasts show about 30% to 50% less ascorbate, while total cellular ascorbate remains similar to wild type. 3. Reduced chloroplastic ascorbate in pht4;4 mutants is associated with diminished NPQ/de-epoxidation index and extensive metabolome remodeling without detectable oxidative stress.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced NPQ and xanthophyll de-epoxidation under high light; no major stress symptoms under normal light.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to modulate chloroplast ascorbate partitioning and downstream metabolic state, but caution is needed because reduced chloroplastic ascorbate compromises photoprotective NPQ under high light.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Untargeted metabolomics (Zenodo accession not specified in text); chloroplast thermal proteome/PISA dataset in PRIDE: PXD052756.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper suggests PHT4;4 is an ascorbate transporter rather than a biosynthetic enzyme or TF. Partial phenotype implies additional chloroplastic ascorbate transporter(s) may exist, with PHT4;2 proposed as a candidate background factor.

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: First committed step; key control step for ascorbate biosynthesis as described in the paper.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms precursor in the biosynthetic pathway leading to ascorbate; controls overall ascorbate pool.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of VTC2 strongly lowers total and chloroplastic ascorbate, causing extensive metabolome remodeling and reduced NPQ/de-epoxidation, without oxidative stress under normal light.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: VTC2 transcript detected in all genotypes except vtc2-4 mutant.
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosol-associated biosynthetic pathway contribution; paper notes final step of ascorbate biosynthesis occurs in mitochondria.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: vtc2-4 mutant analysis; RT-PCR; HPLC quantification of total and chloroplastic ascorbate; nonaqueous fractionation; chlorophyll fluorescence/NPQ; untargeted metabolomics.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Normal light (~80 µmol photons m^-2 s^-1); high light (~800 µmol photons m^-2 s^-1 for 2 days).
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VTC2 encodes GDP-L-galactose phosphorylase, the first committed step of ascorbate biosynthesis. 2. vtc2-4 retains only about 20% of wild-type total ascorbate and about 10% of wild-type chloroplastic ascorbate. 3. Severe ascorbate deficiency alters amino acid, nucleotide salvage, and secondary metabolism in the absence of detectable oxidative stress.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced NPQ and de-epoxidation index, slightly enhanced high-light sensitivity.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key engineering target for boosting ascorbate content and chloroplast metabolic homeostasis, but strong loss-of-function impairs photoprotection under high light.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Untargeted metabolomics (Zenodo accession not specified in text); comparative physiological and biochemical profiling.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper uses VTC2 primarily as an ascorbate-deficient biosynthesis mutant to compare with chloroplast transport deficiency.

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as an important control point; discussed as associated with growth, starch accumulation, and chilling tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Sedoheptulose-1,7-bisphosphate; in vitro alternative substrate fructose-1,6-bisphosphate was used in the assay
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Sedoheptulose-7-phosphate; in vitro fructose-6-phosphate from alternative substrate assay
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Dephosphorylates sedoheptulose-1,7-bisphosphate in the Calvin–Benson cycle; recombinant assay monitored phosphatase activity using fructose-1,6-bisphosphate as alternative substrate.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Modulates carbon fixation and precursor supply that can influence broad primary metabolism rather than directly forming ascorbate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Ascorbate directly decreases SBPase thermal stability and inhibits recombinant SBPase activity by ~57%, supporting a mechanistic link between chloroplastic ascorbate and metabolic regulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: PISA thermal proteome profiling; recombinant protein expression in E. coli; in vitro enzyme activity assay with and without ascorbate.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SBPase is one of the strongest chloroplastic protein candidates affected by ascorbate in the PISA assay. 2. Ascorbate decreases SBPase thermostability. 3. 10 mM ascorbate significantly inhibits recombinant SBPase activity, indicating a direct metabolite–enzyme regulatory interaction.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced SBPase activity could constrain Calvin-cycle flux, growth, and starch accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important caution point when engineering chloroplast ascorbate levels, because elevated ascorbate may suppress SBPase activity and alter carbon assimilation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although not genetically manipulated in planta, SBPase is experimentally validated as an ascorbate-responsive enzyme central to the paper’s mechanistic conclusions.
